courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Reading Plays: Asian and Asian American Theater
courses.description: Reading Plays guides students in close readings of dramatic literature, in particular of plays that are typically left out of the traditional theatrical canon. Students will learn about the socio-historical context of each play, and in-class exercises will introduce them to the foundation of script analysis: they will examine the play’s given circumstances, dialogue, dramatic action, characters, and style. Students will read a new play every week; assignments include weekly in-class writing exercises and short critical papers. Some playwrights we may explore are: Ayad Akhtar, Jaclyn Backhaus, Christopher Chen, Mashuq Mushtaq Deen, Prince Gomolvilas, David Henry Hwang, Naomi Iizuka, Rajiv Joseph, Hansol Jung, Haruna Lee, Kimber Lee, Young Jean Lee, Qui Nguyen, Jiehae Park, Lloyd Suh, Kristina Wong, Lauren Yee, and Chay Yew.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: Internationalism or U.S. Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Reading Plays: Black Theater
courses.description: Reading Plays guides students in the close reading of dramatic literature, focusing specifically on plays that are often overlooked in the traditional theatrical canon. This Fall 2025 edition will emphasize texts from both the classic and contemporary Black Theatre canon. Students will learn about the socio-historical context of each play, and in-class exercises will introduce them to the fundamentals of script analysis. They will examine the play’s given circumstances, structure, dialogue, dramatic questions, characters, and style. Each week, we will focus on a different playwright to deepen our understanding of American theatre through the lens of Black and African American artists. Students will write weekly reflections to enhance their ability to articulate how the plays and playwrights we study address the Black experience in America. For their midterm and final projects, students will curate a theatrical season based on the plays we’ve read and discussed in class. These papers will explore how the plays relate to one another by reflecting on history, sharing cultural narratives, and examining the ongoing impact of their content on today’s audience.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Acting
courses.description: This course introduces students to the art of acting. They will acquire fundamental acting skills and engage in artistic experimentation. Students will learn how to create a repeatable score of psychophysical actions, script analysis, use the body/voice as the actor’s performing instrument; explore internal (memory, impulses, and so forth) and external (the other actors, the environment, objects, and so forth) stimuli as creative sources; rediscover of play and imagination; and develop work ethics and discipline. Students in this course will engage in physical activity and be required to spend 3-5 hours in rehearsals outside of scheduled class time. First year students are encouraged to enroll.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Acting
courses.description: This course introduces students to the art of acting. They will acquire fundamental acting skills and engage in artistic experimentation. Students will learn how to create a repeatable score of psychophysical actions, script analysis, use the body/voice as the actor’s performing instrument; explore internal (memory, impulses, and so forth) and external (the other actors, the environment, objects, and so forth) stimuli as creative sources; rediscover of play and imagination; and develop work ethics and discipline. Students in this course will engage in physical activity and be required to spend 3-5 hours in rehearsals outside of scheduled class time. First year students are encouraged to enroll.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Beginning Dances of the African Diaspora: Brazilian Capoeira, Samba, and Maculelê
courses.description: This dynamic course invites students to dive into the vibrant world of dances of the African Diaspora, where movement, music, rhythm, and history come together. In 2025-26, students will explore the rich cultural heritage of the African Diaspora in Brazil, especially the forms Capoeira, Samba, and Maculelê. The course begins with energizing warm-up exercises designed to prepare the body for the flow and intensity of these dance forms. Through embodied practice, students will develop foundational skills, strengthen the connection between dance and music, learn about diasporic history, and engage in community building. Readings include “Capoeira’s Incredible Roots: Brazil’s Unique Art Form,” “The Cultural History of Brazilian Samba Music,” and “Maculele, history of this lesser known martial arts from Brazil,” among others. May be repeated for credit. No experience necessary. Letter grading. Appropriate for first years.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Dance in the United States: A Cultural History
courses.description: This course focuses on dance in the United States from the early 20th century to today with attention to how it has interacted with the nation’s cultural transformations. The historical study of dance as an embodied art form inevitably lends itself to an intersectional examination of how gender, race, ethnicity, and class experiences continually reflect and shape individual as well as collective identities. We will examine dance forms that have grown in and outside of concert halls, discuss seminal texts in dance and cultural studies, analyze dance videos and live performances, and write short papers that critically consider the relationship between the histories of American dance and culture over the last century.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WP

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Beginning Ballet
courses.description: This is a beginning ballet class designed for students who have never had ballet before. The goal is to demonstrate fundamental understanding and execution of basic ballet technique and to develop a foundation in movement that will carry over and support any other style of dance or physical practice. It will include barre and center work, across-the-floor combinations, and stretching and strengthening practices. Correct alignment, coordination, and body mechanics will be emphasized. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Voice in Performance: Shakespeare
courses.description: This fall, Voice in Performance will focus on Shakespeare in preparation for the Theater and Dance Department’s production of Romeo and Juliet, directed by Professor Randy Reyes. Students in Voice in Performance will have preference at the time of casting. Students will work to discover their own voice in this class. Habits that have formed in the body will be worked on to free the voice and allow for its true potential to exist. Breathing and relaxation exercises, and developing a sense of body awareness and presence will all be a part of the journey of the class. Improvisation and games will be used as tools to help define openness, balance and creativity, strength and vulnerability. Students will consult various books and memorize a number of texts. Students are asked to write responses to readings, a report on a book of the instructor’s choice, a report on a Macalester theater production, and a review of one professional play. A large part of a student’s grade depends on the thoroughness and rigor of their work, as well as how much they develop and improve their skills throughout the semester. Students are expected to attend and participate in all classes and to complete all assignments. There is one required book for the class, Speaking Shakespeare by Patsy Rodenburg. Frequency: Offered occasionally. Appropriate for first years.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Dance Improvisation
courses.description: This class introduces students to the process of spontaneously creating movement, and the art of composing in the moment. Creative exploration is facilitated using a variety of movement scores and structures, working with the elements of time, space, quality, sensory experiences and perception. This course emphasizes collaboration and community, as well as individual agency within that framework. Through a kinesthetic lens, using curiosity and awareness, we will delve into who we are and how we move through the world. The class includes an introduction to Contact Improvisation, the "art-sport" developed by Steve Paxton in 1972. Come with a desire to move, an open mind and a willingness to explore in a non-competitive environment. Open to students with any level of previous experience in dance and movement, from beginning to advanced. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Fundamentals of Scenography
courses.description: Scenography is the creation of imagined spaces for performance. In this course we will study the fundamentals of scenography holistically, including scenic, lighting, costume, and sound design. Students will develop the ability to analyze and critique elements of performance design, articulate design ideas through written and verbal means, and complete a design project from textual analysis to tangible objects. This course is a prerequisite for intermediate and advanced design courses in Theater and Dance. Department provides course materials. First year students are encouraged to enroll.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Beginning Contemporary Dance
courses.description: This dynamic introductory course invites you to explore contemporary dance, where movement becomes a language and the body transforms into a canvas of expression. Through a fusion of physicality and creativity, you will awaken your body and mind. This course will guide you to acquire foundational dance skills, pushing the boundaries of strength and flexibility, and the fluidity of alignment and flow. Each class is rooted in growth and rediscovery through dance movements. You will refine your physical awareness, discovering new dimensions of the body in motion as you join a community of movers. No experience necessary. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Playwriting
courses.description: In this course, students engage in a series of playwriting exercises and read a wide variety of plays. They will read new and contemporary plays that employ different storytelling techniques (i.e., structure, character arcs, staging elements, etc.), in order to embrace the unlimited possibilities of theatricality, exemplifying why we write for the stage. Students will develop a "playwriting toolkit" as they explore their artistic interests. In-class exercises, prompts, small-group workshopping, and related readings will challenge each writer’s individual development. A mid-term check-in and in-class readings of final plays will allow students to hear their work out loud in a supportive public setting. May be repeated for credit.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Beginning Breaking
courses.description: This course introduces students to fundamental characteristics, techniques, and principles of Breaking/Breakdance. The class will dive into the typical movements, histories and origins of this urban dance style. We are going to deep detail using theories and concepts that can help our minds, bodies and spirit connect further and deeper to the styles themselves. This class will allow your own identity and way of motion to be in the forefront. The course is physically rigorous, and emphasizes community building. We will utilize class time to learn and discuss the history of Breaking and its evolution by reading the book The Birth of Breaking by Serouj "Midus" Aprahamian. No experience required. All are welcome. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Sound Design
courses.description: This course will provide the student with an introduction to the creative and technical process of sound design in contemporary theater. Through lecture, demonstration, and projects, students will learn the aesthetic of sound design, the basic science of sound, audio software, and the process of collaboration. Students will acquire the skills to analyze and research a script; to edit, manipulate, and program sound files; and to present and defend design projects. First-year students with relevant experience are encouraged to enroll.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Stage Management
courses.description: This course will introduce students to the multi-faceted responsibilities of the stage manager in theatrical stage productions. Through a combination of lectures, exercises, site visits and group discussion, students will learn the responsibilities and tools of managing any collaborative creative team. Each student will either Stage Manage or Assistant Stage Manage a THDA Production during the year. By the end of this course, students will have developed core proficiencies in note-taking, information management, giving written & verbal feedback, collaboration, leadership, communication, conflict intervention, decision making and team organization. Students receive 2 credits per semester, but only after two consecutive semesters of enrollment. Appropriate for First Years.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Intermediate Ballet
courses.description: This is a beginning intermediate level ballet class for students who have acquired a foundation in the basic skills of classical ballet or have successfully completed THDA 51. Students will be expected to develop or expand their ballet technique while continuing to grow as dance artists. This class will consist of ballet barre and center work, simple pirouettes and the development of basic petit allegro steps. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Prerequisite(s): THDA 51, 22, or permission of instructor.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Intermediate Contemporary Dance
courses.description: Designed for intermediate to advanced dancers, the class invites you to explore the nuances of alignment, momentum, and articulation, while honing the relationship between movement and musicality. Each session is a dynamic blend of warm-ups, center exercises and dance phrases that challenge both body and mind. Building on the foundation of Beginning Contemporary Dance (or with permission of instructor), this course is a space to refine your expression, precision, and stamina. You will expand your technical skills to let your movement speak with clarity and power. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Prerequisite(s): THDA 41, 24 or permission of instructor.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Performance Histories and Theories
courses.description: By examining key movements in dance and theater history from the late 19th century to the present, this seminar explores the active relationship between theoretical thought and aesthetic innovation on stage. Through close readings of primary, secondary, and critical texts, the course highlights the ways in which dance and theater advanced movement by movement, each “ism” a reaction to what came before, and how the transformation from historical avant-garde to experimental contemporary performance represents dynamic and cumulative ruptures with the mainstream. Ultimately, they shape how we imagine, see, and create dance and theater today. Work in this class will support learning theories and methodologies for a larger project, such as a capstone and Honors thesis, or projects, as well as independent research in theater, dance, and other forms of live performance. Required for Theatre and Dance majors. Frequency: every fall.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Directing for the Stage
courses.description: This course prepares advanced theater students to direct scenes for public performances, and thus focuses on the acquisition of skills such as text analysis, dynamic storytelling, stage composition, and communication. Students will choose a scene from a play proposed by the instructor, research style and aesthetic, facilitate a rehearsal process, and stage a live performance. They will also learn best collaborative practices, since the class unfolds in conversation with the Acting courses (THDA 120) and culminates in public performances at the James A. Williams Theater. Frequency: every fall. Prerequisite(s): THDA 120 or THDA 235 or THDA 360, or permission of the instructor. First-year students with relevant experience are encouraged to enroll. Required for THDA majors.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Directing for the Stage Lab
courses.description: This course prepares advanced theater students to direct scenes for public performances, and thus focuses on the acquisition of skills such as text analysis, dynamic storytelling, stage composition, and communication. Students will choose a scene from a play proposed by the instructor, research style and aesthetic, facilitate a rehearsal process, and stage a live performance. They will also learn best collaborative practices, since the class unfolds in conversation with the Acting courses (THDA 120) and culminates in public performances at the James A. Williams Theater. Frequency: every fall. Prerequisite(s): THDA 120 or THDA 235 or THDA 360, or permission of the instructor.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Directing for the Stage Lab
courses.description: This course prepares advanced theater students to direct scenes for public performances, and thus focuses on the acquisition of skills such as text analysis, dynamic storytelling, stage composition, and communication. Students will choose a scene from a play proposed by the instructor, research style and aesthetic, facilitate a rehearsal process, and stage a live performance. They will also learn best collaborative practices, since the class unfolds in conversation with the Acting courses (THDA 120) and culminates in public performances at the James A. Williams Theater. Frequency: every fall. Prerequisite(s): THDA 120 or THDA 235 or THDA 360, or permission of the instructor.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Francophone Theater of Exile and Immigration
courses.description: In this course, we will examine recent theater and film from all over the francophone world, with a focus on three main topics: the act of writing and performing while living in exile, the idea of the Other on the stage, and the development of colonial and post-colonial subjects in francophone drama. The form of each work that we study will vary widely, from classical theater to minimalist contemporary staging. In addition to literary and cultural analyses, we will explore three different aspects of theater production: acting, directing, and writing, with projects in French for each of those roles.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Advanced Ballet
courses.description: This is an advanced level ballet class for students who have achieved an advanced level of skill and artistry in classical ballet. Students will be expected to expand and develop their ballet technique while continuing to grow as dance artists. It will include barre work, pirouettes ending in all positions, and petit allegro with beats. Combinations will increase in complexity and students will be expected to pick up material quickly. May be repeated for credit. Letter grading. Prerequisite(s): Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Performance Practice in Student Projects
courses.description: Students are involved in Theater and Dance Department productions as actors or dancers in student-led pieces. Performance Practice in Student Projects is the appropriate course when students will not work under direct supervision of a faculty member, but are part of a student director’s or student choreographer’s work in an Honors production or Dance Concert. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. S/N grading only.

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Performance Practice: Theater
courses.description: Students are involved in production as actors or dancers, assistant choreographers or choreographers, assistant directors or directors, assistant designers or designers, dramaturgs and playwrights. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor required; please contact the Theater and Dance Department directly for audition and registration information

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Performance Practice
courses.instructor: Mina KinukawaJill LileMarciano Silva Dos Santos
courses.description: Students are involved in production as actors or dancers, assistant choreographers or choreographers, assistant directors or directors, assistant designers or designers, dramaturgs and playwrights. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor required; please contact the Theater and Dance Department directly for audition and registration information

courses.subject: THDA
courses.title: Technical Practice
courses.description: Students are involved in set, costume, lighting and sound engineering and construction, and running crew. Two credits awarded at the end of the semester. May be repeated for credit. Prerequisite(s): Permission of instructor required; please see instructor to be added to registration roster

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Introduction to Women’s, Gender, and Sexuality Studies
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the range and importance of 20th century as well as current feminist and queer theories and practices to our understanding of positive social change. It will analyze feminist and queer histories of resistance and alternatives to economic and political control, in the U.S. and elsewhere. The framework for the course is the intersection of gender and sexuality with race, class, nationality, and dis/ability; it will address such issues as economic marginalization, social movements, the institutions of family and marriage, migration and the role of the state/nation among others. Depending on the instructor, the course generally focuses on either LGBTQI studies or on transnational perspectives of these issues.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: History of Childhood
courses.description: This course examines the history of childhood and youth in the United States. The historical voices and perspectives of childhood that we study will pay close attention to the significance of race, ethnicity, class,\\ gender, sexuality, time periods, and social settings. Some questions we will consider relevant to the study of history: Do children have agency? What is the role of children as subjects in history? How has childhood been socially and historically constructed? Why are children such galvanizing social and political symbols? How is identity shaped in childhood and what impact does this have on adult society at certain historical moments?
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WC

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Gender, Sexualities and Feminist Visual Culture
courses.description: This course examines the ways in which gender and sexuality are understood in modern visual culture. It also covers a wide range of feminist approaches in the 20th and 21st century art and as they have been articulated in theory. Students explore social constructions of gender and sexualities, their visible and invisible representation, and discuss the impact of feminism and the changing role of women in society. The course will also cover some of the most recent global feminist trends and new directions in the feminist theory. Feminist work from Africa, India, Asia and Eastern and Central Europe and various marginalized cultural centers in Western Europe and the United States will be addressed.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Gender, Sexualities and Feminist Visual Culture
crosslistings: First-Year Course only; cross-listed with ART 252-F1 (12115)
courses.description: This course examines the ways in which gender and sexuality are understood in modern visual culture. It also covers a wide range of feminist approaches in the 20th and 21st century art and as they have been articulated in theory. Students explore social constructions of gender and sexualities, their visible and invisible representation, and discuss the impact of feminism and the changing role of women in society. The course will also cover some of the most recent global feminist trends and new directions in the feminist theory. Feminist work from Africa, India, Asia and Eastern and Central Europe and various marginalized cultural centers in Western Europe and the United States will be addressed.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: The Psychology of Gender
courses.description: This class is an introduction to feminist psychological theory and research dedicated to understanding and critiquing biological, psychological, social, and cultural meanings and implications of gender and its intersections with class, race, physical ability, sexual orientation, etc. Examples of research and theory will come from a wide variety of areas in psychology and related disciplines, and will address such issues as socialization and social development, stereotypes, bodies and body image, social relationships, identity, language, violence, sexuality and sexual behavior, well-being, work, etc. We will also learn about the historical, cultural, and epistemological underpinnings of psychological research on gender. Counts as a UP3 course. Prerequisite(s): PSYC 100 or permission of the instructor.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Health and Power: Disability and Debility in a Globalized World
courses.description: What is “health” in both a globalized world and a world shaped by legacies of colonialism, transatlantic enslavement, climate change, and forced migration? Is “healthy” a politically neutral and universally accessible classification? How do we understand disability - chronic or short term - as not just a biological experience, but a social one shaped by gender, sexuality, race, and empire? In this interdisciplinary class we will look at case studies to try to answer these questions. Some case studies include, but are not limited to, public health and labor migration between the US/Mexico border, medical racism and biological warfare, US imperialism in the Pacific Islands, the War on Gaza, detentions and quarantine in Guantanamo Bay, and South African apartheid. Through these examples, we will discuss the ways that health, medicine, and disability become used to uphold US/European power and white supremacy, and become the gauge for deciding who is worth the classification of “human” and who is deemed not worthy of life.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Education and the End of Empire
crosslistings: First day attendance required; cross-listed with EDUC 294-02 (12292)
courses.description: In this seminar, we will consider how epistemicide and its originary instantiation in formalized educational institutions and academic disciplines both contained the core of empire’s brutal logics and the kernel of empire’s impending collapse. Studying across intellectual traditions and genres, we will develop vigorous dialectic inquiry into this extraordinary contradiction. And we will study some of the vital educational traditions that precede empire, that have continued uninterrupted, and that exceed dialectic relations to modernity through resurgence, the fantastic, and more.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Queer Classics
courses.description: In this course, we will read some of the best novels of the twentieth century—classics of queer literature. We will study how queer writers navigated censorship and discrimination and created radical visions of love, hope, and joy. E.M. Forster wrote a novel about queer love to be published only after his death; Virginia Woolf’s Orlando is a trans character who lives for hundreds of years; and James Baldwin’s Giovanni’s Room takes us to a Parisian gay bar. These queer masterpieces (among others!) paved the way for a dynamic canon of queer literature and theory, and our class will seek to understand the enduring impact of these texts. Students will read deeply and undertake a semester-long zine project.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Endarkened Feminist Epistemologies
courses.description: Endarkened Feminist Epistemology (EFE) is an approach to research that honors the wisdom, memory, spirituality, and critical interventions of transnational Black women’s ways of knowing (Dillard, 2016). A framework created by scholar Dr. Cynthia Dillard, it articulates how reality is known from the vantage point of transnational Black women - transnational recognizing the connections between African descended women throughout the diaspora as well as the continent. Through reflecting on course readings, writing, and sacred practice, this class will explore EFE’s claims and methodologies, as well as center other transnational Black women whose work helps us make connections between history, spirituality, and our collective positionality within academia.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WC

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: LGBTQ Politics and Policy
courses.description: This course offers an intersectional analysis of LGBTQ+ policy in the United States. We will explore key achievements such as federal marriage equality, the repeal of “Don’t Ask, Don’t Tell,” and expanded anti-discrimination protections, while critically examining their impacts on historically marginalized groups—including queer people of color, transgender, gender nonconforming, disabled, and low-income individuals. Additionally, the course acknowledges the recent major setbacks in LGBTQ+ rights policy, drawing on readings from queer liberation scholars and political scientists to evaluate current gains and challenges, and to envision what full equality might look like for LGBTQ+ people.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Queer Christians
courses.description: In this course, students will explore how stories of past and present Christians and the theologies they produced contribute to the histories of gender and sexuality, particularly in the United States. We will pay special attention to the development of and intersections among queer theory, queer and trans activism, queer biblical interpretation, and queer theology in the U.S. from the late twentieth century on. Students will learn how the development of queer biblical interpretation and theology grew out of the intellectual and activist work of feminist, womanist, and mujerista scholars, theologians, ethicists, and pastors. In readings, class discussion, online forums, and written and creative work, students will be invited to explore the social construction of gendered identities and sexualities over time, from antiquity to the postmodern, and the intersections of these identities with religion, race, ethnicity, class, and dis/ability. Although the majority of the class will be devoted to historical scholarship and theological writing that insists on the inclusion of diverse sexualities and genders in the Christian past, present, and future, we will also examine the role that some forms of American Christianity play in the exclusion of LGBTQ+ people, the enforcement of compulsory heterosexuality, the “naturalization” of a gender binary, and the reification of patriarchal, racist, homophobic, and transphobic cultures.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Gender and Japanese Language
courses.description: When it comes to gender and English, people often think about third-person pronouns (they, she, he, ze, etc.), what others call you. In contrast, Japanese language speakers choose from a variety of first-person pronouns, what you call yourself. Some follow societal norms while others subvert them. This course examines how individuals and media creators make such linguistic choices (not just pronouns, but other expressions as well), and how these choices are fluid and based on such diverse factors as age, region, trend, rhetoric, and ideology. A very basic knowledge of Japanese is required, but all readings will be in English. Prerequisite: JAPA102 or permission of instructor.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Worlds Upside Down: Revolutions in Theories and Practices
courses.description: Are we living in revolutionary times? How do we know? This course takes a journey through the last 50 years of some large upheavals across the world-in language, politics, economics, culture, and media-to find some answers of how we are similar and different. It uncovers how and why the struggles, in theories and practices, for power and representation have created the conditions in which we exist today. We travel through all kinds of ’post’ and ’neo’-liberalism, humanism, feminism, nationalism, and colonialism-and how they intertwine in our own lived experiences. Some topics: protest against gender, sexual, race, economic, and linguistic bio-regimes, revolts of the colonized and marginalized, and resistant/ liberatory creations in art, technology, and media. Authors include Linda Tuhiwai Smith, Michel Foucault, Theresa Cha, Silvia Federici, Noam Chomsky, Ta-Nehisi Coates, bell hooks, Chela Sandoval, and Gayatri Spivak, among others. At the end of the course, we will all know a little more about where we stand. People from all disciplines welcome. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore or junior standing or permission of instructor, and at least one course in the department (core course recommended, cross-listed accepted).
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Women, Peace and Security
crosslistings: Permission of instructor required; cross-listed with POLI 324-01 (12673)
courses.description: In 2000, the United Nations Security Council adopted Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security (WPS). This resolution acknowledged the inordinate impact of armed conflict on women and girls, as well as the crucial role that women can and do already play in conflict prevention, conflict resolution, peacekeeping and peacebuilding efforts. It urged states and other actors to increase the participation of women - and to incorporate gender perspectives - in all peace and security efforts. Since 2000, there have been nine additional resolutions that together have created the Women, Peace and Security agenda, a global framework for advancing gender equity in all areas of international peace and security. This course will explore feminist scholarship on armed conflict, peace and security leading up to the adoption of UNSCR 1325, as well as the 20 years of research, policy and practice on Women, Peace and Security. We will focus on the contributions and limitations of the WPS agenda as well as on new themes and issues that have emerged over time. This fall, which marks the 25th anniversary of Resolution 1325 on Women, Peace and Security, students in the course will have the opportunity to travel to New York City for three days of meetings at the United Nations focused on the development and implementation of the WPS agenda. Travel details, including cost, are currently being worked out but will be available to students before the start of the course.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Indigenous Feminisms
crosslistings: AMST 394-02 (12859); registration limit adjusted to save 2 seats for First Year students
courses.description: This course provides an interdisciplinary mechanism for exploring Indigenous feminisms and queer Indigenous studies. We will explore Indigenous histories through women’s, feminist, two-spirit, and queer writings that raise critical questions about normative gender and sexuality, nationhood, colonization, and decolonization. We will also explore the following topics: the relationship between mainstream feminism and Indigenous feminism; gender and sexual violence; settler colonialism and decolonization; law and restorative justice; sexuality; two-spirit, queer, and gender non-conforming subjectivities and politics; sexual and reproductive health; activism; the environment, embodiment, and materialisms; culture and tradition; erotics; biopolitics; Indigenous epistemologies; and sovereignty and governance.
courses.gen_ed_requirements: U.S Identities and Differences, Writing WA

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: LGBTQ+ Health
courses.description: This is an advanced psychology course on the health of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, and other sexually and gender diverse (LGBTQ+) people. The course is designed to engage students in a critical examination of health and healthcare disparities among LGBTQ+ populations, and evidence-based health models that explain the root causes and drivers of disparities. Students will also learn about and gain experience in behavioral interventions: how these are researched, developed, and implemented to target disparities, and promote the health and wellbeing of LGBTQ+ populations. Class time will consist of didactic instruction, large and small group discussions, application activities, and student presentations.

courses.subject: WGSS
courses.title: Black Feminisms and Visual Thinking Against Erasure
crosslistings: First day attendance required; cross-listed with MCST 388-01 (12521)
courses.description: This course examines visual methods, particularly those employed by Black women artists and thinkers work in visual practices as forms of resistance, survival, and world-building. Through critical engagement with photography, film, installation art, and experimental media, we explore how Black feminist visual strategies challenge dominant regimes of representation while imagining otherwise possibilities. The course traces visual genealogies that refuse the violence of hypervisibility while simultaneously confronting erasure, navigating what Saidiya Hartman calls "the visual field of black captivity and its afterlife." Students will analyze works by artists who transform the very grammar of visuality, developing critical frameworks to understand how Black feminist aesthetics rework time, space, and embodiment. Through both analytical and creative projects, we will consider how these visual practices serve as methods of theorizing that expand beyond conventional academic boundaries, offering provocations for imagining and enacting more liberatory futures.
